title: The Future of Indexing: Passive Investing Isn't Passive
gist: Research Affiliates' Rob Arnott and Lillian Wu argue that 'passive' market-cap-weighted index funds embed systematic hidden costs by buying recent winners at a premium and selling recent losers at a discount; they propose construction tweaks—smoothing market cap over five-year averages, applying fundamental metrics, and adding banding/seasoning rules—to achieve higher returns with lower turnover.

themes.theme: Hidden Costs of Cap-Weighted Passive Indexing

insights.claim: Research Affiliates argues that 'passive' market-cap-weighted index funds are not neutral: they embed hidden trading costs by systematically paying a premium for recent winners (at additions) and selling recent losers at a discount (at deletions)—making 'passive' an active bet on price momentum at every rebalance.
insights.speaker: Rob Arnott
insights.so_what: The 'passive is neutral' narrative is empirically false at the margin: index funds systematically trade at a structural disadvantage—adding overvalued stocks and selling undervalued ones—creating a persistent, quantifiable drag on returns relative to alternatives that avoid this trading pattern.
insights.quote: "Passive" market-cap-weighted index funds are not as neutral as they seem and often embed hidden costs within their stock-selection mechanism, paying a premium for recent winners and selling recent losers at a discount.

insights.claim: Research Affiliates proposes ameliorating cap-weighting's shortcomings through: (1) smoothing market capitalization through five-year averages, (2) aligning index eligibility with fundamental metrics of business scale such as sales and book value, and (3) adding banding and seasoning rules—preserving rules-based transparency while improving return and lowering turnover.
insights.speaker: Rob Arnott
insights.so_what: The proposed tweaks represent a practical middle ground between pure passive and active—offering the cost transparency of indexing while systematically avoiding the momentum-chasing costs; a direct justification for fundamental indexing (RAFI) products over standard cap-weighted ETFs.
insights.quote: Cap weighting's shortcomings can be ameliorated and its benefits maximized through simple tweaks to index construction, by, for example, smoothing market capitalization through five-year averages and aligning index eligibility with fundamental metrics of business scale, such as sales and book value.
